title: Public inheritance and substitution

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Public inheritance and substitution; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Public inheritance models substitutability: derived objects must satisfy the base contract, preserve invariants, and respect access and construction rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Circle publicly implements a Shape drawing and area contract so code using Shape references can accept it without special cases. Your goal is to evaluate whether derived objects safely substitute for a base interface. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Class interface and invariants; unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access; Composition and delegation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Public inheritance models substitutability: derived objects must satisfy the base contract, preserve invariants, and respect access and construction rules. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Private inheritance is implementation reuse, multiple inheritance adds ambiguity, base constructors run first, and nonvirtual operations do not provide runtime replacement. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Any class that reuses another class's code should publicly inherit from it.

includes: Use inheritance only for a valid is-a relationship and maintain base-class contracts.
Evaluate whether derived objects safely substitute for a base interface.
excludes: Private inheritance is implementation reuse, multiple inheritance adds ambiguity, base constructors run first, and nonvirtual operations do not provide runtime replacement.

outcomes: Evaluate whether derived objects safely substitute for a base interface.

examples.title: Public inheritance and substitution: defined case
examples.situation: A Circle publicly implements a Shape drawing and area contract so code using Shape references can accept it without special cases.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A Circle publicly implements a Shape drawing and area contract so code using Shape references can accept it without special cases.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Public inheritance requires is-a substitutability, not mere code reuse; otherwise prefer composition or a private implementation technique.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Public inheritance requires is-a substitutability, not mere code reuse; otherwise prefer composition or a private implementation technique.

examples.title: Public inheritance and substitution: boundary case
examples.situation: A Stack does not inherit publicly from vector because exposing arbitrary vector insertion would violate the stack interface.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A Stack does not inherit publicly from vector because exposing arbitrary vector insertion would violate the stack interface.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Private inheritance is implementation reuse, multiple inheritance adds ambiguity, base constructors run first, and nonvirtual operations do not provide runtime replacement.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Any class that reuses another class's code should publicly inherit from it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Evaluate whether derived objects safely substitute for a base interface.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any class that reuses another class's code should publicly inherit from it.
misconceptions.correction: Public inheritance requires is-a substitutability, not mere code reuse; otherwise prefer composition or a private implementation technique.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Any class that reuses another class's code should publicly inherit from it.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Public inheritance and substitution: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Private inheritance is implementation reuse, multiple inheritance adds ambiguity, base constructors run first, and nonvirtual operations do not provide runtime replacement.

recap.summary: You should now be able to evaluate whether derived objects safely substitute for a base interface. Keep this boundary in view: Private inheritance is implementation reuse, multiple inheritance adds ambiguity, base constructors run first, and nonvirtual operations do not provide runtime replacement.
